title: Epicureans, Cynics, and Stoics
essence: Vervaeke frames the Hellenistic period as a civilizational meaning crisis precipitated by Alexander the Great's conquests, which destroyed the polis, the self-contained political and cultural community in which Greek identity was embedded. The result was domicide on a civilizational scale: millions of people whose identity had been constituted by their place in a civic community suddenly found themselves displaced, cosmopolitan, and existentially homeless. Three philosophical movements arose as therapeutic responses. The Epicureans, pioneered by Epicurus, withdrew from politics into small communities of friendship, built a hedonic calculus of pleasure management, and practiced philosophical medicine, the 'call no man a philosopher who has not alleviated the suffering of others' criterion. The Cynics radically stripped down: Diogenes performed a kind of philosophical theater of nakedness, demonstrating that what people claim to need (honor, wealth, status) is illusion, and that genuine purity of motivation is all that matters. Zeno of Citium, influenced by the Cynics but reconstructing something more social, founded Stoicism: a school that re-grounded identity not in polis membership but in participation in the universal logos, the rational principle structuring the cosmos, through the psycho-technology of co-identification, by which the individual identifies with the rational order rather than contingent social roles. Each school addresses the same problem of domicide through a different re-grounding strategy: pleasure management, purity of will, or logos-identification.
keyClaims: Alexander the Great's conquests destroyed the polis, producing civilizational domicide, the loss of the cultural home in which Greek identity was constituted.
The Epicureans responded with therapeutic friendship communities and hedonic calculus, treating philosophy explicitly as medicine for existential suffering.
The Cynics stripped identity down to purity of motivation, performing philosophical theater to expose the illusoriness of conventional social goods.
Zeno founded Stoicism by re-grounding identity in the universal logos, the rational principle structuring the cosmos, enabling meaningful existence even without a particular community or home.

All three schools are responses to the same problem of domicide and existential displacement, each offering a different psycho-technology for re-establishing the agent-arena relationship after the collapse of the polis.

concepts.summary: Named and defined as the destruction of home, not merely physical but cultural: the loss of the community, history, and shared meaning-making in which identity is embedded.
Each Hellenistic school is analyzed as offering a different psycho-technology: hedonic calculus (Epicureans), nakedness/theater (Cynics), co-identification with logos (Stoics).

The Hellenistic crisis is framed as what happens after the axial revolution: the new interiority and universalism of axial thought come under pressure when the institutional supports that structured them collapse.

The polis-destruction problem is analyzed as an agent-arena disruption: without the polis, the Greek's identity as agent no longer has a matching arena. All three schools offer different ways to re-establish the coupling.
The existential displacement of the Hellenistic period creates exactly the conditions for parasitic processing, untethered identity, cognitive flexibility overwhelmed by options, no shared frame.

Each school defines wisdom differently: Epicurean wisdom as managing pleasure/pain; Cynic wisdom as stripping to purity; Stoic wisdom as aligning with logos, but all share the criterion of alleviating existential suffering.
The Cynic critique is a direct attack on having-mode existence: conventional goods (honor, wealth, status) are named as false being-mode satisfiers that produce modal confusion.
The Hellenistic period is presented as the first large-scale modern-style meaning crisis, the parallel to modernity after the death of God is made explicit.

people.summary: The historical agent of civilizational domicide: his conquests destroyed the polis and produced the Hellenistic cosmopolitan displacement that necessitated the new therapeutic philosophies.

Founder of Epicureanism: therapeutic philosopher of friendship communities, hedonic calculus, and philosophical medicine. His criterion, 'Call no man a philosopher who has not alleviated the suffering of others', is quoted.
Paradigmatic Cynic: performed philosophical theater of radical stripping-down to expose the illusoriness of conventional goods and the purity of natural motivation.
Founder of Stoicism: influenced by Cynics but reconstructed a social philosophy grounding identity in the universal logos rather than contingent community membership.
Co-author (with Smith) of the domicide concept that Vervaeke imports to characterize the Hellenistic civilizational disruption.
Walsh's theological use of domicide as a bridge between the Hellenistic and modern crises is invoked to frame the episode's central analogy.

crossRefs.context: Parasitic processing from ep-13 now gets its historical instantiation: the Hellenistic crisis is the civilizational version of the cognitive trap that Buddhism analyzed at the individual level.
Stoic practice is developed in detail in ep-15 through Marcus Aurelius, extending the co-identification psycho-technology into a full daily spiritual regimen.
The Hebraic axial revolution from ep-3 is now contrasted with the Hellenistic response: the Hebrews had narrative identity through history; the Greeks lost civic identity through conquest.

quotableMoments.quote: Domicide is the destruction of home. Now there's two ways in which Domicide can occur. One, of course, is physical destruction of your house, and that's important, but there's also Cultural Domicide in which you have a house, you have a dwelling, but it is not very much your home.
quotableMoments.context: Introduction of domicide as the key concept for understanding the Hellenistic meaning crisis and its modern parallel.
